# Gidugu Venkata Ramamurthy

### World Telugu Day

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Aug 29, 2024

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!LX7Q!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F94eadbf5-06ef-4ce5-8544-41b70bbf8716_220x326.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!LX7Q!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F94eadbf5-06ef-4ce5-8544-41b70bbf8716_220x326.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Gidugu Venkata Ramamurthy, a linguist and social activist, who championed the cause of Vyavharika Bhaasha( used by common man) over the Grandhika Bhaasha( scholarly language), in whose honor World Telugu Day is celebrated.

He was born on August 29, 1863 at Parvathapeta currently in Srikakulam district, right near to the border with Odisha on the banks of the Vamsadhara river.

Orphaned young, with his father Veeraraju passing when he was just 12, Gidugu’s life was shaped by struggle and quiet determination. Raised by his elder sister, he pursued education privately.

His career began at **Gajapati Maharaja High School, Parlakimidi**, where he not only taught but began sowing the seeds of linguistic reform. Parlakimidi, nestled in the cultural confluence of Andhra and Odisha, became the crucible for his ideas on accessibility and inclusivity in language.

  
In his pursuit to understand the roots of Telugu and its evolution, he immersed himself in the **Vizianagaram Maharaja’s library**, poring over rare manuscripts and comparative language texts. These weren’t just academic indulgences,they were tools for decoding the **Sasanas** (stone inscriptions), which held the civilizational memory of the region.

He studied **multiple scripts**,Telugu, Sanskrit, Prakrit, and tribal languages.to unlock the syntax of history. His work wasn’t limited to translation, he explored the **philosophy of language** itself, how meaning is shaped, how power is encoded, and how accessibility transforms pedagogy.

  
In the remote agency areas near **Parlakimidi**, he immersed himself in the lives of the **Savara (Sora)** tribal community, a people whose language had no formal script, and whose oral traditions risked being lost to time. Rather than study them from afar, he **walked into their forests**, lived among them, and listene, not just to their words, but to their worldview.

He **developed a script** for the Savara language, giving it a written form for the first time.He compiled a **lexicon and grammar**, preserving linguistic structures that had only existed orally.

His work culminated in *A Manual of the So:ra (or Savara) Language*, published in 1931, a pioneering ethnolinguistic document.These efforts were so intense and prolonged that his **excessive use of quinine** during forest travels led to hearing loss, a sacrifice made in service of linguistic dignity.

His insistence on Vyavaharika Bhasha, *spoken Telugu* as the medium of instruction was revolutionary in a time when *Grandhika Bhāṣa*, the Sanskritized version, dominated education, literature, and inscriptions. His critique was not merely linguistic, it was **pedagogical, philosophical, and democratic**.

The very inscriptions (*Sasanas*) that recorded royal decrees, temple grants, and historical events were unreadable to the people they affected. He argued that **language must serve comprehension**, not prestige.

His writings and speeches between 1910-14 in media and publications emphasized that instruction in Vyavahārika Telugu would, improve literacy and retention,bridge the gap between oral tradition and formal education and restore dignity to the everyday speaker.

His advocacy for *Vyavahārika Bhāṣa* as the medium of instruction wasn’t a polite academic proposa, it was a **grassroots revolution**. He stood against the gatekeepers of *Grandhika Telugu*, who saw Sanskritized prose as the only legitimate form of scholarship. But he knew. **if language cannot be understood, it cannot liberate**.

Between **1910–1914**, he launched a full blown campaign. Published essays, pamphlets, and letters in journals like *Vivāda Tarangini*, *Saraswati*, and *Andhra Bhāṣā*.

Delivered fiery speeches on public platforms, dramatizing the absurdity of teaching children in a language they couldn’t speak.

Engaged in debates with pundits and professors, often turning their own logic against them with wit and clarity. Used satire and storytelling to show how *Grandhika Telugu* alienated the very people it claimed to uplift.

  
His works like *Balakavisaranyam*, *Gadya Chintamani*, *Andhra Pandita Bhishakkula Bhasha Bheshajam*, and *Vyasavali* weren’t just texts,they were **manifestos**.

Each one modeled how *Vyavahārika Telugu* could be elegant, expressive, and pedagogically sound, served as a counterpoint to the ornate, inaccessible *Grandhika style* and became tools for teachers, reformers, and writers to adopt a more inclusive linguistic approach.

**Kandukuri Veeresalingam**, inspired by Gidugu’s vision, founded the *Vartamana Vyavaharikandhra Bhasha Parivartaka Samajam*,a society dedicated to propagating spoken Telugu as the literary norm.

**Chilukuri Narayan Rao**, through the *Abhinavandhra Kavi Panditasabha*, passed a landmark resolution in **1933** declaring that *Vyavahārika Bhāṣa* must be the **medium of instruction**,a formal recognition of Gidugu’s decades-long struggle.

In **1936**, a journal began publishing articles in *Vyavahārika Telugu*, marking the formal entry of spoken language into literary discourse. In **1937**, **Tapi Dharma Rao**, a reformist writer and thinker, launched a periodical in the same style, further legitimizing the movement.

These efforts, combined with decades of Gidugu’s advocacy, led to a **historic shift**, **Government and universities** officially adopted *Vyavahārika Bhāṣa* as the **medium of instruction and examination**,a policy that continues to this day.

He also wrote Kalinga Charitra,a historical work chronicling the **ancient and medieval history of Odisha**, especially the cultural and political legacy of the Kalinga region. It reflects his deep engagement with regional histories and their linguistic textures.

And one of his most monumental works, a Sora-English dictionary in 1938, that formalized the **Savara language** into a structured lexicon, bridging tribal orality with global scholarship. Published by the **Government Press**, it spans **318 pages**, offering phonetics, grammar, and cultural context. As well as Savara Patalu,a c**ompilation of tribal poems**, preserving the lyrical soul of the Savara people.

Gidugu Venkata Ramamurthy Gaaru, was a linguist, historian, lexicographer, thinker, a towering intellectual, who championed the cause of spoken Telugu, a truly great soul.
